title: Rhodes

infobox.value: ([/roʊdz/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-us-roads.ogg "File:En-us-roads.ogg"); [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language"): Ρόδος, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Greek "Romanization of Greek"): *Ródos* [[ˈroðos]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Greek "Help:IPA/Greek")) is the largest of [Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece")'s [Dodecanese](/wiki/Dodecanese "Dodecanese") islands and their historical capital; it is the [ninth largest island](/wiki/List_of_islands_in_the_Mediterranean#By_area "List of islands in the Mediterranean") in the [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea"). Administratively, Rhodes constitutes a separate municipality within the [Rhodes regional unit](/wiki/Rhodes_(regional_unit) "Rhodes (regional unit)"), which is part of the [South Aegean](/wiki/South_Aegean "South Aegean") [administrative region](/wiki/Administrative_regions_of_Greece "Administrative regions of Greece"). The principal town of the island and seat of the municipality is the [city of Rhodes](/wiki/Rhodes_(city) "Rhodes (city)"),[[1]](#cite_note-Kallikratis-1) home to 50,636 inhabitants according to the 2011 census. By 2022, the island’s population had grown to 125,113 people.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Located northeast of [Crete](/wiki/Crete "Crete") and southeast of [Athens](/wiki/Athens "Athens"), Rhodes is often referred to by several nicknames: the "Island of the Sun" after its patron sun god [Helios](/wiki/Helios "Helios"); "The Pearl Island"; and "The Island of the Knights", a reference to the [Knights of Saint John of Jerusalem](/wiki/Knights_Hospitaller "Knights Hospitaller"), who ruled the island from 1310 to 1522.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

[![General view of the village of Lindos, with the acropolis and beaches, island of Rhodes, Greece.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/Lindos_Rhodes_1.jpg/250px-Lindos_Rhodes_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lindos_Rhodes_1.jpg)

General view of the village of Lindos, with the acropolis and beaches, island of Rhodes, Greece

**Rhodes** ([/roʊdz/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-us-roads.ogg "File:En-us-roads.ogg"); [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language"): Ρόδος, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Greek "Romanization of Greek"): *Ródos* [[ˈroðos]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Greek "Help:IPA/Greek")) is the largest of [Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece")'s [Dodecanese](/wiki/Dodecanese "Dodecanese") islands and their historical capital; it is the [ninth largest island](/wiki/List_of_islands_in_the_Mediterranean#By_area "List of islands in the Mediterranean") in the [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea"). Administratively, Rhodes constitutes a separate municipality within the [Rhodes regional unit](/wiki/Rhodes_(regional_unit) "Rhodes (regional unit)"), which is part of the [South Aegean](/wiki/South_Aegean "South Aegean") [administrative region](/wiki/Administrative_regions_of_Greece "Administrative regions of Greece"). The principal town of the island and seat of the municipality is the [city of Rhodes](/wiki/Rhodes_(city) "Rhodes (city)"),[[1]](#cite_note-Kallikratis-1) home to 50,636 inhabitants according to the 2011 census. By 2022, the island’s population had grown to 125,113 people.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Located northeast of [Crete](/wiki/Crete "Crete") and southeast of [Athens](/wiki/Athens "Athens"), Rhodes is often referred to by several nicknames: the "Island of the Sun" after its patron sun god [Helios](/wiki/Helios "Helios"); "The Pearl Island"; and "The Island of the Knights", a reference to the [Knights of Saint John of Jerusalem](/wiki/Knights_Hospitaller "Knights Hospitaller"), who ruled the island from 1310 to 1522.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Historically, Rhodes was famous for the [Colossus of Rhodes](/wiki/Colossus_of_Rhodes "Colossus of Rhodes"), one of the [Seven Wonders of the Ancient World](/wiki/Seven_Wonders_of_the_Ancient_World "Seven Wonders of the Ancient World"). [The Medieval Old Town of the City of Rhodes](/wiki/Fortifications_of_Rhodes "Fortifications of Rhodes") has been declared a [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site"). During the early 21st century the island was one of the most popular tourist destinations in Europe.

## Name

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rhodes&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Name")]

The island has been known as Ρόδος (Ródos) in Greek throughout its history. Similar-sounding ῥόδον (*rhódon*) in ancient Greek was the word for the rose, whilst in modern Greek the also similar-sounding ρόδι (*ródi*) or ρόιδο (*róido*) refers to the pomegranate. It was also called Lindos ([Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_language "Ancient Greek language"): Λίνδος).[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) In addition, the island has been called *Rodi* in [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"), *Rodos* in [Turkish](/wiki/Turkish_language "Turkish language"), and רודי (Rodi) or רודיס (Rodes) in [Ladino](/wiki/Ladino_language "Ladino language").

Other ancient names were Ρόδη (Rodē), Τελχινίς ([Telchinis](/wiki/Telchines "Telchines")) and Ηλιάς (Helias).

*[The Travels of Sir John Mandeville](/wiki/The_Travels_of_Sir_John_Mandeville "The Travels of Sir John Mandeville")* incorrectly reports that Rhodes was formerly called "Collosus", through a conflation of the [Colossus of Rhodes](/wiki/Colossus_of_Rhodes "Colossus of Rhodes") and [Paul](/wiki/Paul_the_Apostle "Paul the Apostle")'s *[Epistle to the Colossians](/wiki/Epistle_to_the_Colossians "Epistle to the Colossians")*, which refers to [Colossae](/wiki/Colossae "Colossae").[[6]](#cite_note-6)

The island's name might be derived from *erod*, [Phoenician](/wiki/Phoenician_language "Phoenician language") for snake, since the island was home to many snakes in antiquity.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rhodes&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

### Prehistory

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rhodes&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Prehistory")]

During the [Late Pleistocene](/wiki/Late_Pleistocene "Late Pleistocene"), the island was inhabited by an unnamed species of [dwarf elephant](/wiki/Dwarf_elephant "Dwarf elephant").[[8]](#cite_note-:0-8) The island has been inhabited by humans since at least the late [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic"), as evidenced by remains found at Kalythies cave on the northeast of the island.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)

### Early Bronze Age

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rhodes&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Early Bronze Age")]

At the end of the [3rd millennium BC](/wiki/3rd_millennium_BC "3rd millennium BC"), during the Early [Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze_Age "Bronze Age"), major urban settlements began to develop on Rhodes, such as Asomatos, which is the earliest known urban centre on the island. Duck shaped vases found at Asomatos suggest contact with Cyprus as well as elsewhere in the Aegean region during this time.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

### Middle Bronze Age

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rhodes&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Middle Bronze Age")]

#### Minoan period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rhodes&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Minoan period")]

Main article: [Minoan civilization](/wiki/Minoan_civilization "Minoan civilization")

The [Minoan Civilisation](/wiki/Minoan_Civilisation "Minoan Civilisation") established a settlement at Tiranda on the northwest of the island during the [16th century BC](/wiki/16th_century_BC "16th century BC"), presumably to facilitate trade.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

### Late Bronze Age

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rhodes&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Late Bronze Age")]

#### Mycenaean period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rhodes&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Mycenaean period")]

Main articles: [Doric Hexapolis](/wiki/Doric_Hexapolis "Doric Hexapolis"), [Mycenaean Greece](/wiki/Mycenaean_Greece "Mycenaean Greece"), and [Greek Dark Ages](/wiki/Greek_Dark_Ages "Greek Dark Ages")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/Carnelian_necklace_As_7702.jpg/250px-Carnelian_necklace_As_7702.jpg)](/wiki/File:Carnelian_necklace_As_7702.jpg)

Mycenaean necklace of carnelian found in [Kattavia](/wiki/Kattavia "Kattavia")

In the 15th century BC, [Mycenaean Greeks](/wiki/Mycenaean_Greece "Mycenaean Greece") invaded. After the [Bronze Age collapse](/wiki/Bronze_Age_collapse "Bronze Age collapse"), the first renewed outside contacts were with [Cyprus](/wiki/Ancient_history_of_Cyprus "Ancient history of Cyprus").[[13]](#cite_note-13)

In Greek legend, Rhodes was claimed to have participated in the [Trojan War](/wiki/Trojan_War "Trojan War") under the leadership of [Tlepolemus](/wiki/Tlepolemus "Tlepolemus").[[14]](#cite_note-14)

### Iron Age

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rhodes&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Iron Age")]

Main article: [Archaic Greece](/wiki/Archaic_Greece "Archaic Greece")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/Warrior_head_vases_Ashmolean%2C_Camirus%2C_Rhodes%2C_590-575_BC.jpg/250px-Warrior_head_vases_Ashmolean%2C_Camirus%2C_Rhodes%2C_590-575_BC.jpg)](/wiki/File:Warrior_head_vases_Ashmolean,_Camirus,_Rhodes,_590-575_BC.jpg)

Warrior-headed vase, [Camirus](/wiki/Camirus "Camirus"), Rhodes, 590–575 BC

In the 8th century BC, the island's settlements started to form, with the coming of the [Dorians](/wiki/Dorians "Dorians"), who built the three important cities of [Lindus](/wiki/Lindus "Lindus"), [Ialysus](/wiki/Ialysus "Ialysus") and [Camirus](/wiki/Camirus "Camirus"), which together with [Kos](/wiki/Kos "Kos"), [Cnidus](/wiki/Knidos "Knidos") and [Halicarnassus](/wiki/Halicarnassus "Halicarnassus") (on the mainland) made up the so-called [Dorian Hexapolis](/wiki/Doric_hexapolis "Doric hexapolis") (Greek for six cities).

In [Pindar](/wiki/Pindar "Pindar")'s ode, the island was said to be born of the union of [Helios](/wiki/Helios "Helios") the sun god and the nymph [Rhodos](/wiki/Rhodos "Rhodos"), and the cities were named for their three sons. The *rhoda* is a pink [hibiscus](/wiki/Hibiscus "Hibiscus"), native to the island. [Diodorus Siculus](/wiki/Diodorus_Siculus "Diodorus Siculus") added that [Actis](/wiki/Actis_(mythology) "Actis (mythology)"), one of the sons of Helios and Rhode, travelled to [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt"). He built the city of [Heliopolis](/wiki/Heliopolis_(Ancient_Egypt) "Heliopolis (Ancient Egypt)") and taught the Egyptians [astrology](/wiki/Astrology "Astrology").[[15]](#cite_note-15)

In the second half of the 8th century BC, the sanctuary of Athena received votive gifts that are markers for cultural contacts: small ivories from the Near East and bronze objects from Syria. At [Kameiros](/wiki/Kameiros "Kameiros") on the northwest coast, a former Bronze Age site, At the site where the temple was established in the 8th century BC, archaeologists have identified an additional contemporaneous sequence of carved ivory figurines. The cemeteries of Kameiros and Ialyssos have also produced several notable examples of Orientalizing Rhodian jewelry, which are generally dated to the 7th and early 6th centuries BC.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

### Classical Age

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rhodes&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Classical Age")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/Apollon_temple_acropolis_Rhodes.jpg/250px-Apollon_temple_acropolis_Rhodes.jpg)](/wiki/File:Apollon_temple_acropolis_Rhodes.jpg)

Temple of Apollo at the [Acropolis of Rhodes](/wiki/Acropolis_of_Rhodes "Acropolis of Rhodes")

Main article: [Classical Greece](/wiki/Classical_Greece "Classical Greece")

#### Persian period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rhodes&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Persian period")]

The [Persians](/wiki/Achaemenid_Empire "Achaemenid Empire") invaded and overran the island, but they were in turn defeated by forces from [Athens](/wiki/Classical_Athens "Classical Athens") in 478 BC. The Rhodian cities joined the [Athenian League](/wiki/Delian_League "Delian League"). When the [Peloponnesian War](/wiki/Peloponnesian_War "Peloponnesian War") broke out in 431 BC, Rhodes remained largely neutral, although it remained a member of the League. The war lasted until 404 BC, but by this time Rhodes had withdrawn entirely from the conflict and decided to go their own way.

Being the eastern gate to the Aegean Sea, Rhodes was an important stopping point for [Phoenician](/wiki/Phoenicians "Phoenicians") merchants, and prosperous trading colonies and Phoenician communities emerged there, some within the Greek cities.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

In 408 BC, the cities [united to form one territory](/wiki/Synoecism "Synoecism"). They built the [city of Rhodes](/wiki/City_of_Rhodes "City of Rhodes"), a new capital on the northern end of the island. Its regular plan was, according to [Strabo](/wiki/Strabo "Strabo"), superintended by the Athenian architect [Hippodamus](/wiki/Hippodamus "Hippodamus").

In 357 BC, the island was conquered by the king [Mausolus](/wiki/Mausolus "Mausolus") of [Caria](/wiki/Caria "Caria"); then it fell again to the Persians in 340 BC. Their rule was also short.

#### Hellenistic period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rhodes&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Hellenistic period")]

Main articles: [Hellenistic Greece](/wiki/Hellenistic_Greece "Hellenistic Greece") and [Greece in the Roman era](/wiki/Greece_in_the_Roman_era "Greece in the Roman era")

Rhodes then became a part of the growing empire of [Alexander the Great](/wiki/Alexander_the_Great "Alexander the Great") in 332 BC, after he defeated the Persians.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/Colosse_de_Rhodes_%28Barclay%29.jpg/250px-Colosse_de_Rhodes_%28Barclay%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Colosse_de_Rhodes_(Barclay).jpg)

The [Colossus of Rhodes](/wiki/Colossus_of_Rhodes "Colossus of Rhodes"), as depicted in an artist's impression of 1880

Following the death of Alexander, his generals ([Diadochi](/wiki/Diadochi "Diadochi")) vied for control of the kingdom. Three — [Ptolemy](/wiki/Ptolemy_I_Soter "Ptolemy I Soter"), [Seleucus](/wiki/Seleucus_I_Nicator "Seleucus I Nicator"), and [Antigonus](/wiki/Antigonus_I_Monophthalmus "Antigonus I Monophthalmus") — succeeded in dividing the kingdom among themselves. Rhodes formed strong commercial and cultural ties with the Ptolemies in [Alexandria](/wiki/Alexandria "Alexandria"), and together formed the Rhodo-Egyptian alliance that controlled trade throughout the Aegean in the 3rd century BC.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

The city developed into a maritime, commercial and cultural center; its coins circulated nearly everywhere in the Mediterranean. Its famous schools of philosophy, science, literature and [rhetoric](/wiki/Rhetoric "Rhetoric") shared masters with Alexandria: the Athenian rhetorician [Aeschines](/wiki/Aeschines "Aeschines"), who formed a school at Rhodes; [Apollonius of Rhodes](/wiki/Apollonius_of_Rhodes "Apollonius of Rhodes"), who wrote about [Jason](/wiki/Jason "Jason") and [Medea](/wiki/Medea "Medea") in the *[Argonautica](/wiki/Argonautica "Argonautica")*; the observations and works of the astronomers [Hipparchus](/wiki/Hipparchus "Hipparchus") and [Geminus](/wiki/Geminus "Geminus"); and the rhetorician [Dionysius Thrax](/wiki/Dionysius_Thrax "Dionysius Thrax"). Its school of sculptors developed, under [Pergamese](/wiki/Pergamon "Pergamon") influence, a rich, dramatic style that can be characterized as "[Hellenistic](/wiki/Hellenistic_civilization "Hellenistic civilization") [Baroque](/wiki/Baroque "Baroque")". [Agesander of Rhodes](/wiki/Agesander_of_Rhodes "Agesander of Rhodes"), with two other Rhodian sculptors, carved the famous *[Laocoön group](/wiki/Laoco%C3%B6n_and_His_Sons "Laocoön and His Sons")*, now in the [Vatican Museums](/wiki/Vatican_Museums "Vatican Museums"), and the [large sculptures rediscovered at Sperlonga](/wiki/Sperlonga_sculptures "Sperlonga sculptures") in the villa of [Tiberius](/wiki/Tiberius "Tiberius"), probably in the early [Imperial period](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire").[[19]](#cite_note-19)

In 305 BC, Antigonus directed his son, [Demetrius](/wiki/Demetrius_I_of_Macedon "Demetrius I of Macedon"), to [besiege Rhodes](/wiki/Siege_of_Rhodes_(305%E2%80%93304_BC) "Siege of Rhodes (305–304 BC)") in an attempt to break its alliance with Egypt. Demetrius created huge [siege engines](/wiki/Siege_engines "Siege engines"), including a 180 ft (55 m) [battering ram](/wiki/Battering_ram "Battering ram") and a [siege tower](/wiki/Siege_tower "Siege tower") called [Helepolis](/wiki/Helepolis "Helepolis") that weighed 360,000 lb (163,293 kg). Despite this engagement, in 304 BC after only one year, he relented and signed a peace agreement, leaving behind a huge store of military equipment. The Rhodians sold the equipment and used the money to erect a statue of their sun god, [Helios](/wiki/Helios "Helios"), the statue since called the [Colossus of Rhodes](/wiki/Colossus_of_Rhodes "Colossus of Rhodes"). The Rhodians celebrated in honour of Helios a grand festival, the [Halieia](/wiki/Halieia "Halieia").[[20]](#cite_note-20)

Throughout the 3rd century BC, Rhodes attempted to secure its independence and commerce, particularly its virtual control over the [grain trade](/wiki/Grain_trade "Grain trade") in the eastern Mediterranean. Both of these goals depended on none of the three great Hellenistic states achieving dominance. Consequently, the Rhodians pursued a policy of maintaining a balance of power among the [Antigonids](/wiki/Antigonid_dynasty "Antigonid dynasty"), [Seleucids](/wiki/Seleucid_dynasty "Seleucid dynasty"), and [Ptolemies](/wiki/Ptolemaic_dynasty "Ptolemaic dynasty"), even if that meant going to war with its traditional ally, [Ptolemaic Egypt](/wiki/Ptolemaic_Egypt "Ptolemaic Egypt"). To this end, they employed their economy and their excellent navy as leverage, which was manned by proverbially the finest sailors in the Mediterranean world: “If we have ten Rhodians, we have ten ships.”[[21]](#cite_note-21)

The Rhodians also established their dominance on the shores of [Caria](/wiki/Caria "Caria") across from their island, which became known as the "[Rhodian Peraia](/wiki/Rhodian_Peraia "Rhodian Peraia")". It extended roughly from the modern city of [Muğla](/wiki/Mu%C4%9Fla "Muğla") (ancient *Mobolla*) in the north and [Kaunos](/wiki/Kaunos "Kaunos") bordering [Lycia](/wiki/Lycia "Lycia") in the south, near the present-day [Dalyan](/wiki/Dalyan "Dalyan"), Turkey.

Rhodes successfully carried on this policy through the course of the third century BC, an impressive achievement for what was essentially a democratic state. By the end of that period, however, the balance of power was crumbling, as declining Ptolemaic power made Egypt an attractive target for Seleucid ambitions. In 203/2 BC the young and dynamic kings of [Antigonid Macedon](/wiki/Antigonid_Macedon "Antigonid Macedon") and [Seleucid Empire](/wiki/Seleucid_Empire "Seleucid Empire"), [Philip V](/wiki/Philip_V_of_Macedon "Philip V of Macedon") and [Antiochus III](/wiki/Antiochus_III "Antiochus III"), agreed to accept—at least temporarily—their respective military ambitions: Philip's [campaign in the Aegean](/wiki/Cretan_War_(205%E2%80%93200_BC)#War_against_Pergamum_and_Rhodes "Cretan War (205–200 BC)") and [western Anatolia](/wiki/Cretan_War_(205%E2%80%93200_BC)#Asia_Minor_campaign "Cretan War (205–200 BC)") and Antiochus's plan for Egypt. Heading a coalition of small states, the Rhodians [checked Philip's navy](/wiki/Battle_of_Chios_(201_BC) "Battle of Chios (201 BC)"), but not his superior army. Without a third power to which to turn, the Rhodians (along with ambassadors from the [kingdom of Pergamum](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Pergamum "Kingdom of Pergamum"), the [Ptolemaic kingdom](/wiki/Ptolemaic_kingdom "Ptolemaic kingdom"), and Athens) appealed in 201 BC to the [Roman Republic](/wiki/Roman_Republic "Roman Republic").[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/Rhodos_Ritterkastell_bei_Lindos.jpg/250px-Rhodos_Ritterkastell_bei_Lindos.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rhodos_Ritterkastell_bei_Lindos.jpg)

Medieval gate at the Acropolis of [Lindos](/wiki/Lindos "Lindos")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/Rhodes_88-42_BC-AD.jpg/250px-Rhodes_88-42_BC-AD.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rhodes_88-42_BC-AD.jpg)

Silver [drachma](/wiki/Ancient_drachma "Ancient drachma") of Rhodes, 88/42 BC. Obverse: radiate head of [Helios](/wiki/Helios "Helios"). Reverse: [rose](/wiki/Rose "Rose"), *"rhodon"* (ῥόδον), the symbol of Rhodes.

Despite being exhausted by the [Second Punic War](/wiki/Second_Punic_War "Second Punic War") against [Hannibal](/wiki/Hannibal "Hannibal") (218–201 BC) the Romans agreed to intervene, still angry over the Macedonian alliance with Carthage that had led to the [First Macedonian War](/wiki/First_Macedonian_War "First Macedonian War") from 214 to 205 BC. The Senate saw the appeal from Rhodes and her allies as the opportunity to pressure Philip. The result was the [Second Macedonian War](/wiki/Second_Macedonian_War "Second Macedonian War") (200–196 BC), which Rome won and greatly reduced Macedon's power, prestige, and territory. Rhodian independence was preserved. Rhodian influence in the Aegean was cemented through the organization of the [Cyclades](/wiki/Cyclades "Cyclades") into the [Second Nesiotic League](/wiki/Second_Nesiotic_League "Second Nesiotic League") under Rhodian leadership.

The Romans withdrew from Greece after the end of the conflict, but the resulting power vacuum quickly drew in Antiochus III and subsequently the Romans. The [Roman–Seleucid War](/wiki/Roman%E2%80%93Seleucid_War "Roman–Seleucid War") lasted from 192 to 188 BC with Rome, Rhodes, Pergamon, and other Roman-allied Greek states defeated the Seleucids and their allies, the last Mediterranean power that might even vaguely threaten Roman dominance. Having provided Rome with valuable naval help in her first foray into Asia, the Rhodians were rewarded with territory and enhanced status by the [Treaty of Apamea](/wiki/Treaty_of_Apamea "Treaty of Apamea") (188 BC).[[24]](#cite_note-24) The Romans once again evacuated the east – the Senate preferred clients to provinces – but it was clear that Rome now ruled the Mediterranean and Rhodian autonomy was ultimately dependent upon good relations with them.

Those good graces soon evaporated in the wake of the [Third Macedonian War](/wiki/Third_Macedonian_War "Third Macedonian War") (171–168 BC). In 169 BC, during the war against [Perseus](/wiki/Perseus_of_Macedon "Perseus of Macedon"), Rhodes sent [Agepolis](/wiki/Agepolis "Agepolis") as ambassador to the consul [Quintus Marcius Philippus](/wiki/Quintus_Marcius_Philippus_(consul_186_BC) "Quintus Marcius Philippus (consul 186 BC)"), and then to Rome in the following year, hoping to turn the Senate against the war.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

Rhodes remained scrupulously neutral during the war, but in the view of hostile elements in the Senate she had been a bit too friendly with the defeated King Perseus. Some actually proposed declaring war on the island republic, but this was averted.

#### Roman period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rhodes&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Roman period")]

In 164 BC, Rhodes became a "permanent ally" of Rome, which was essentially a reduction to [client state](/wiki/Client_state "Client state") of nominal but meaningless independence. It was said that the Romans ultimately turned against the Rhodians because the islanders were the only people they had encountered who were more arrogant than themselves.

After surrendering its independence, Rhodes became a cultural and educational center for Roman noble families. It was especially noted for its teachers of rhetoric, such as [Hermagoras](/wiki/Hermagoras_of_Temnos "Hermagoras of Temnos") and the unknown author of *[Rhetorica ad Herennium](/wiki/Rhetorica_ad_Herennium "Rhetorica ad Herennium")*. At first, the state was an important ally of Rome and enjoyed numerous privileges, but these were later lost in various machinations of Roman politics. [Cassius](/wiki/Gaius_Cassius_Longinus "Gaius Cassius Longinus") eventually invaded the island and sacked the city in 43 BC. In the early Imperial period Rhodes became a favorite place for political exiles.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

In the 1st century AD, the Emperor [Tiberius](/wiki/Tiberius "Tiberius") spent a brief term of exile on Rhodes. By tradition, [Paul the Apostle](/wiki/Paul_the_Apostle "Paul the Apostle") evangelized and helped establish an [early Christian](/wiki/Early_Christianity "Early Christianity") church on the island during the first century.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

In ancient times there was a Roman saying: "[Hic Rhodus, hic salta](/wiki/Hic_Rhodus,_hic_salta "Hic Rhodus, hic salta")!"—"Here is Rhodes, jump here!" (as translated from Ancient Greek "Αὐτοῦ γὰρ καὶ Ῥόδος καὶ πήδημα"), an admonition to prove one's idle boasts by deed, rather than boastful talk. It comes from an [Aesop's fable](/wiki/Aesop%27s_fable "Aesop's fable") called "[The Boasting Traveller](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Three_Hundred_%C3%86sop%27s_Fables/The_Boasting_Traveller "s:Three Hundred Æsop's Fables/The Boasting Traveller")" and was cited by [Hegel](/wiki/Hegel "Hegel"), [Marx](/wiki/Marx "Marx"), and [Kierkegaard](/wiki/Kierkegaard "Kierkegaard").

### Middle Ages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rhodes&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Middle Ages")]

#### Byzantine period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rhodes&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Byzantine period")]

Main article: [Byzantine Greece](/wiki/Byzantine_Greece "Byzantine Greece")

In 395 with the division of the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire"), the long [Byzantine](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire") period began for Rhodes. In [Late Antiquity](/wiki/Late_Antiquity "Late Antiquity"), the island was the capital of the [Roman province](/wiki/Roman_province "Roman province") of the [Islands](/wiki/Islands_(Roman_province) "Islands (Roman province)"), headed by a *[praeses](/wiki/Praeses "Praeses")* (*[hegemon](/wiki/Hegemon "Hegemon")* in Greek), and encompassing most of the [Aegean islands](/wiki/Aegean_islands "Aegean islands"), with twenty cities. Correspondingly, the island was also the [metropolis](/wiki/Metropolis_of_Rhodes "Metropolis of Rhodes") of the ecclesiastical province of Cyclades, with eleven [suffragan](/wiki/Suffragan "Suffragan") sees.[[28]](#cite_note-ODB-28)

Beginning from ca. 600 AD, its influence in maritime issues was manifested in the collection of [maritime laws](/wiki/Admiralty_law "Admiralty law") known as "[Rhodian Sea Law](/wiki/Rhodian_Sea_Law "Rhodian Sea Law")" (*Nomos Rhodion Nautikos*), accepted throughout the Mediterranean and in use throughout Byzantine times (and influencing the development of [admiralty law](/wiki/Admiralty_law#History "Admiralty law") up to the present).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In 622/3, during the climactic [Byzantine–Sasanian War of 602–628](/wiki/Byzantine%E2%80%93Sasanian_War_of_602%E2%80%93628 "Byzantine–Sasanian War of 602–628"), Rhodes was captured by the [Sasanian navy](/wiki/Sasanian_navy "Sasanian navy").[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKia2016223-29)[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGreatrexLieu2005197-30)[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoward-Johnston200633-31)

Rhodes was occupied by the Islamic [Umayyad](/wiki/Umayyad_Caliphate "Umayyad Caliphate") forces of Caliph [Muawiyah I](/wiki/Muawiyah_I "Muawiyah I") in 654, who carried off the remains of the Colossus of Rhodes.[[28]](#cite_note-ODB-28)[[32]](#cite_note-32) The island was again captured by the Arabs in 673 as part of their [first attack on Constantinople](/wiki/Siege_of_Constantinople_(674%E2%80%93678) "Siege of Constantinople (674–678)"). When their fleet was destroyed by [Greek fire](/wiki/Greek_fire "Greek fire") before Constantinople and by storms on its return trip, however, the Umayyads evacuated their troops in 679/80 as part of the Byzantine–Umayyad peace treaty.[[33]](#cite_note-33) In 715 the Byzantine fleet dispatched against the Arabs launched a rebellion at Rhodes, which led to the installation of [Theodosios III](/wiki/Theodosios_III "Theodosios III") on the Byzantine throne.[[28]](#cite_note-ODB-28)[[34]](#cite_note-34)

From the early 8th to the 12th centuries, Rhodes belonged to the [Cibyrrhaeot Theme](/wiki/Cibyrrhaeot_Theme "Cibyrrhaeot Theme") of the Byzantine Empire, and was a centre for shipbuilding and commerce.[[28]](#cite_note-ODB-28) In c. 1090, it was occupied by the forces of the [Seljuk Turks](/wiki/Seljuk_dynasty "Seljuk dynasty"), after the long period of chaos resulting from the [Battle of Manzikert](/wiki/Battle_of_Manzikert "Battle of Manzikert").[[35]](#cite_note-35) Rhodes was recaptured by the Emperor [Alexios I Komnenos](/wiki/Alexios_I_Komnenos "Alexios I Komnenos") during the [First Crusade](/wiki/First_Crusade "First Crusade").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Rhodes-TerrePleinSpain.JPG/250px-Rhodes-TerrePleinSpain.JPG)](/wiki/File:Rhodes-TerrePleinSpain.JPG)

Part of the late medieval [fortifications of Rhodes](/wiki/Fortifications_of_Rhodes "Fortifications of Rhodes")

As Byzantine central power weakened under the [Angeloi](/wiki/Angeloi "Angeloi") emperors (1185–1204), in the first half of the 13th century, Rhodes became the centre of an independent domain under [Leo Gabalas](/wiki/Leo_Gabalas "Leo Gabalas") and his brother [John](/wiki/John_Gabalas "John Gabalas"),[[28]](#cite_note-ODB-28) until it was [occupied](/wiki/Genoese_occupation_of_Rhodes "Genoese occupation of Rhodes") by the [Genoese](/wiki/Republic_of_Genoa "Republic of Genoa") in 1248–1250. The Genoese were evicted by the [Empire of Nicaea](/wiki/Empire_of_Nicaea "Empire of Nicaea"), after which the island became a regular province of the Nicaean state (and after 1261 of the restored Byzantine Empire). In 1305, the island was given as a fief to [Andrea Morisco](/wiki/Andrea_Morisco "Andrea Morisco"), a Genoese adventurer who had entered Byzantine service.

#### Crusader period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rhodes&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Crusader period")]

Further information: [Ottoman Greece](/wiki/Ottoman_Greece "Ottoman Greece") and [Hospitaller Rhodes](/wiki/Hospitaller_Rhodes "Hospitaller Rhodes")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/OttomanJanissariesAndDefendingKnightsOfStJohnSiegeOfRhodes1522.jpg/250px-OttomanJanissariesAndDefendingKnightsOfStJohnSiegeOfRhodes1522.jpg)](/wiki/File:OttomanJanissariesAndDefendingKnightsOfStJohnSiegeOfRhodes1522.jpg)

Ottoman [Janissaries](/wiki/Janissaries "Janissaries") and defending [Knights of Saint John](/wiki/Knights_of_Saint_John "Knights of Saint John") at the [Siege of Rhodes](/wiki/Siege_of_Rhodes_(1522) "Siege of Rhodes (1522)") in 1522, from an Ottoman manuscript

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/A_small_harbour_in_Rhodes.jpg/250px-A_small_harbour_in_Rhodes.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_small_harbour_in_Rhodes.jpg)

Rhodes in the 19th century

In 1306–1310, the Byzantine era of the island's history came to an end when [the island was occupied](/wiki/Hospitaller_conquest_of_Rhodes "Hospitaller conquest of Rhodes") by the [Knights Hospitaller](/wiki/Knights_Hospitaller "Knights Hospitaller").[[28]](#cite_note-ODB-28) Under the rule of the newly named "Knights of Rhodes", the city was rebuilt into a model of the European medieval ideal. Many of the city's famous monuments, including the [Palace of the Grand Master](/wiki/Palace_of_the_Grand_Master_of_the_Knights_of_Rhodes "Palace of the Grand Master of the Knights of Rhodes"), were built during this period.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Maltan_knights_castle_in_rh.jpg/250px-Maltan_knights_castle_in_rh.jpg)](/wiki/File:Maltan_knights_castle_in_rh.jpg)

[Palace of the Grand Master](/wiki/Palace_of_the_Grand_Master_of_the_Knights_of_Rhodes "Palace of the Grand Master of the Knights of Rhodes") in the city of Rhodes

The walls had been strengthened by the Knights through hiring Italian engineers that used knowledge of ballistics to design defences against gunpowder attacks, and which withstood the attacks of the [Sultan of Egypt](/wiki/Sultan_of_Egypt "Sultan of Egypt") in 1444, and a [siege](/wiki/Siege_of_Rhodes_(1480) "Siege of Rhodes (1480)") by the [Ottomans](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") under [Mehmed II](/wiki/Mehmed_II "Mehmed II") in 1480.

#### Ottoman period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rhodes&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Ottoman period")]

Eventually, however, Rhodes [fell](/wiki/Siege_of_Rhodes_(1522) "Siege of Rhodes (1522)") to the large army of [Suleiman the Magnificent](/wiki/Suleiman_the_Magnificent "Suleiman the Magnificent") in December 1522. The Sultan deployed 400 ships delivering 100,000 men to the island (200,000 in other sources). Against this force the Knights, under Grand Master [Philippe Villiers de L'Isle-Adam](/wiki/Philippe_Villiers_de_L%27Isle-Adam "Philippe Villiers de L'Isle-Adam"), had about 7,000 men-at-arms, with an English, Spanish, French, and Italian contingent each defending separate areas and their fortifications. The siege lasted six months, at the end of which the surviving defeated Hospitallers were allowed to withdraw to the [Kingdom of Sicily](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Sicily "Kingdom of Sicily"). Despite the defeat, both Christians and Muslims seem to have regarded the conduct of Villiers de L'Isle-Adam as extremely valiant, and the Grand Master was proclaimed a Defender of the Faith by Pope Adrian VI (see [Knights of Cyprus and Rhodes](/wiki/Knights_Hospitaller#Knights_of_Cyprus_and_Rhodes "Knights Hospitaller")). The knights would later move their base of operations to [Malta](/wiki/History_of_Malta_under_the_Order_of_Saint_John "History of Malta under the Order of Saint John") and [Gozo](/wiki/Gozo "Gozo").

Rhodes was thereafter a possession of the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") (see [Sanjak of Rhodes](/wiki/Sanjak_of_Rhodes "Sanjak of Rhodes")) for nearly four centuries.

### Modern Age

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rhodes&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Modern Age")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/Rhodus_type_M%C3%BCller_A86.jpg/250px-Rhodus_type_M%C3%BCller_A86.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rhodus_type_M%C3%BCller_A86.jpg)

5 soldi Austrian Levant stamp cancelled in brown *RHODUS*.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Rhodes_Italian_landing.jpg/250px-Rhodes_Italian_landing.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rhodes_Italian_landing.jpg)

Italian landing at Rhodes on May 4, 1912, during the Italo-Turkish War

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/Offices_of_the_Prefecture_of_the_Dodecanese_02.jpg/250px-Offices_of_the_Prefecture_of_the_Dodecanese_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Offices_of_the_Prefecture_of_the_Dodecanese_02.jpg)

*Palazzo Governale* (today the offices of the Prefecture of the Dodecanese), built during the [Italian period](/wiki/Italian_Islands_of_the_Aegean "Italian Islands of the Aegean")

In the 19th century the island was populated by ethnic groups from the surrounding nations, including Jews, whose presence goes back 2,300 years.[[37]](#cite_note-auto-37) Under Ottoman rule, they generally did fairly well, but discrimination and bigotry occasionally arose. In February 1840, the Jews of Rhodes were falsely accused by the Greek Orthodox community of ritually murdering a Christian boy. This became known as the [Rhodes blood libel](/wiki/Rhodes_blood_libel "Rhodes blood libel").

Austria opened a post-office at *RHODUS* (Venetian name) before 1864,[[38]](#cite_note-38) as witnessed by stamps with [Franz Joseph](/wiki/Franz_Joseph_I_of_Austria "Franz Joseph I of Austria")'s head.

In 1912, Italy seized Rhodes from the Ottomans during the [Italo-Turkish War](/wiki/Italo-Turkish_War "Italo-Turkish War"). Being under Italian administration, the island's population was thus spared the ["exchange of the minorities"](/wiki/Population_exchange_between_Greece_and_Turkey "Population exchange between Greece and Turkey") between Greece and [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey"). Rhodes and the rest of the Dodecanese Islands were assigned to Italy in the Treaty of Ouchy. Although the treaty stipulated that the islands were to be returned to Turkey, the advent of [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") prevented this from happening. Turkey ceded them officially to Italy with the 1923 [Treaty of Lausanne](/wiki/Treaty_of_Lausanne "Treaty of Lausanne"). It then became the core of their possession of the *[Isole Italiane dell'Egeo](/wiki/Isole_Italiane_dell%27Egeo "Isole Italiane dell'Egeo")*.

Thousands of [Italian colonists](/wiki/Italian_colonists_in_the_Dodecanese "Italian colonists in the Dodecanese") settled in the island, mainly in the capital "Rodi", while some of them founded farm villages (like "Peveragno Rodio" (1929), "Campochiaro" (1935), "San Marco" (1936) and "Savona" (1938): in 1940 the creation of the "[Provincia italiana di Rodi](/wiki/Italian_Islands_of_the_Aegean "Italian Islands of the Aegean")" in the Dodecanese islands was officially proposed. In the late 1930s, Mussolini embarked on a program of [Italianization](/wiki/Italianization "Italianization"), attempting to make the island of Rhodes a transportation hub that would facilitate the spread of Italian culture in [Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece") and the [Levant](/wiki/Levant "Levant"). The [Fascist](/wiki/Italian_Fascism "Italian Fascism") program coincided with improvements to infrastructure, building imposing buildings such as the Hotel Rodon, the Puccini Theater and many administrative buildings with master architects such as [Armando Bernabiti](/wiki/Armando_Bernabiti "Armando Bernabiti") and [Florestano Di Fausto](/wiki/Florestano_Di_Fausto "Florestano Di Fausto").[[39]](#cite_note-39) While the government worked at modernization, they also obliterated many historical buildings that did not match their ideal of a modern society.

The island suffered through many "governors" appointed by the Italian government. As part of this, in 1938, the "Leggi razziali" (Racial Laws) were passed, mimicking the footsteps of the antisemitic policies promoted in other European countries. All Jews who served in the government, including the military, were forced to resign, school children were forced to abandon their studies, and all commerce that included any dealings with Jews was forbidden.

Following the [Italian Armistice of 8 September 1943](/wiki/Armistice_between_Italy_and_Allied_armed_forces "Armistice between Italy and Allied armed forces"), the British attempted to get the Italian garrison on Rhodes to change sides. This was anticipated by the [German Army](/wiki/German_Army_(Wehrmacht) "German Army (Wehrmacht)"), which succeeded in occupying the island with the [Battle of Rhodes](/wiki/Battle_of_Rhodes_(1943) "Battle of Rhodes (1943)"). In great measure, the German occupation caused the British failure in the subsequent [Dodecanese Campaign](/wiki/Dodecanese_Campaign "Dodecanese Campaign").

After September 1943, the Jews were sent to concentration camps. However, the [Turkish](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey") Consul [Selahattin Ülkümen](/wiki/Selahattin_%C3%9Clk%C3%BCmen "Selahattin Ülkümen") succeeded, at considerable risk to himself and his family, in saving 42 Jewish families, about 200 persons in total, who had Turkish citizenship or were members of Turkish citizens' families.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/The_Liberation_of_Rhodes%2C_1945_E30950.jpg/250px-The_Liberation_of_Rhodes%2C_1945_E30950.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Liberation_of_Rhodes,_1945_E30950.jpg)

Indian soldiers taking over a sentry post from a German soldier following the German surrender in 1945

On 8 May 1945, the Germans under [Otto Wagener](/wiki/Otto_Wagener "Otto Wagener") surrendered Rhodes as well as the Dodecanese as a whole to the British, who soon after then occupied the islands as a military protectorate.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

At the [Paris Peace Treaties](/wiki/Paris_Peace_Treaties,_1947#Italy "Paris Peace Treaties, 1947"), Rhodes, together with the other islands of the [Dodecanese](/wiki/Dodecanese "Dodecanese"), was united with [Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece") in February 1947. 6,000 Italian colonists were forced to abandon the island and returned to Italy.

#### Contemporary period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rhodes&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Contemporary period")]

In 1949, Rhodes was the venue for negotiations between [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel") and [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt"), [Jordan](/wiki/Jordan "Jordan"), [Lebanon](/wiki/Lebanon "Lebanon"), and [Syria](/wiki/Syria "Syria"), concluding with the [1949 Armistice Agreements](/wiki/1949_Armistice_Agreements "1949 Armistice Agreements").
[[41]](#cite_note-41)

In 2023, the island was hit by the [2023 Greece wildfires](/wiki/2023_Greece_wildfires "2023 Greece wildfires"), which forced the largest evacuations in the history of Greece. Nearly 19,000 people had to evacuate.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rhodes&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Geography")]

The island of Rhodes is shaped like a [spearhead](/wiki/Spear "Spear"), 79.7 km (49.5 mi) long and 38 km (24 mi) across at its widest, with a total area of approximately 1,400 km2 (541 sq mi) and a coastline of approximately 220 km (137 mi). [Limestone](/wiki/Limestone "Limestone") is the main bedrock.[[43]](#cite_note-43) The [city of Rhodes](/wiki/Rhodes,_Greece "Rhodes, Greece") is located at the northern tip of the island, as well as the site of the ancient and modern commercial [harbours](/wiki/Harbour "Harbour"). The main airport is the [Diagoras International Airport](/wiki/Diagoras_Airport "Diagoras Airport") (IATA code: RHO), located 14 km (9 mi) to the southwest of the city in [Paradisi](/wiki/Paradisi,_Greece "Paradisi, Greece"). The road network radiates from the city along the east and west coasts.

Outside the city of Rhodes, the island is dotted with small villages of whitewashed homes and spa resorts, among them [Faliraki](/wiki/Faliraki "Faliraki"), [Lindos](/wiki/Lindos "Lindos"), [Kremasti](/wiki/Kremasti "Kremasti"), [Haraki](/wiki/Haraki "Haraki"), [Pefkos](/wiki/Pefkos "Pefkos"), [Archangelos](/wiki/Archangelos,_Rhodes "Archangelos, Rhodes"), [Afantou](/wiki/Afantou "Afantou"), [Koskinou](/wiki/Koskinou "Koskinou"), [Embona](/wiki/Embona "Embona") (Attavyros), [Paradisi](/wiki/Paradisi,_Greece "Paradisi, Greece"), and [Trianta](/wiki/Trianta "Trianta") (Ialysos).

Rhodes is situated 363 km (226 mi) east-southeast from the Greek mainland, and 18 km (11 mi) from the southern shore of [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey"). Mount [Attavyros](/wiki/Attavyros "Attavyros"), at 1,216 m (3,990 ft), is the island's highest point of elevation.

### Flora

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rhodes&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Flora")]

Further information: [Natural history of Rhodes](/wiki/Natural_history_of_Rhodes "Natural history of Rhodes")

The interior of the island is mountainous, sparsely inhabited and covered with forests of [pine](/wiki/Pine "Pine") (*[Pinus brutia](/wiki/Pinus_brutia "Pinus brutia")*) and [cypress](/wiki/Cupressus_sempervirens "Cupressus sempervirens") (*Cupressus sempervirens*). While the shores are rocky, the island has arable strips of land where [citrus](/wiki/Citrus "Citrus") fruit, [wine](/wiki/Wine "Wine") grapes, vegetables, [olives](/wiki/Olives "Olives") and other crops are grown. Many flowering plants for which the island is named are abundant.

### Fauna

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rhodes&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Fauna")]

Further information: [Natural history of Rhodes](/wiki/Natural_history_of_Rhodes "Natural history of Rhodes")

The Rhodian population of [fallow deer](/wiki/Fallow_deer "Fallow deer") was found to be genetically distinct in 2005, and to be of urgent conservation concern.[[44]](#cite_note-44) In [Petaloudes](/wiki/Petaloudes "Petaloudes") Valley (Greek for "Valley of the Butterflies"), large numbers of [tiger moths](/wiki/Euplagia_quadripunctaria "Euplagia quadripunctaria") gather during the summer months.

### Earthquakes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rhodes&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Earthquakes")]

Earthquakes include the [226 BC earthquake](/wiki/226_BC_Rhodes_earthquake "226 BC Rhodes earthquake") that destroyed the [Colossus of Rhodes](/wiki/Colossus_of_Rhodes "Colossus of Rhodes"); [one on 3 May 1481](/wiki/1481_Rhodes_earthquake "1481 Rhodes earthquake") which destroyed much of the city of Rhodes;[[45]](#cite_note-45) and one on 26 June 1926.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

On 15 July 2008, Rhodes was struck by a [6.3 magnitude earthquake](/wiki/2008_Dodecanese_earthquake "2008 Dodecanese earthquake"), causing minor damage to a few old buildings and one death.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rhodes&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Climate")]

Rhodes has a hot-summer [Mediterranean climate](/wiki/Mediterranean_climate "Mediterranean climate") (*Csa* in the [Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification")) with mild winters and hot summers. The South East of the island experiences a significantly warmer climate with [Lindos](/wiki/Lindos "Lindos") registering a mean annual temperature of around 22.0 °C (71.6 °F),[[48]](#cite_note-[[National_Observatory_of_Athens]]-48) making it the warmest area in Greece.[[49]](#cite_note-National_Observatory_of_Athens-49)[[50]](#cite_note-Iefimerida-50) The [Rhodes International Airport](/wiki/Rhodes_International_Airport "Rhodes International Airport") in coastal [Paradeisi](/wiki/Paradeisi "Paradeisi") has never dropped below 1.2 °C (34.2 °F) from 1977 when the station started its operation.[[51]](#cite_note-51)[[52]](#cite_note-52) The old inland [Maritsa Airport](/wiki/Rhodes_Air_Base "Rhodes Air Base") has dropped as low as −4.0 °C (24.8 °F).[[53]](#cite_note-53) The highest temperature ever recorded in Paradeisi was 40.2 °C (104.4 °F) while the highest temperature ever recorded on the island was 43.6 °C (110.5 °F) in [Lindos](/wiki/Lindos "Lindos").[[54]](#cite_note-54)[[55]](#cite_note-55) Coastal Rhodes falls in 11a [hardiness zone](/wiki/Hardiness_zone "Hardiness zone").[[56]](#cite_note-56) Moreover, according to the [Hellenic National Meteorological Service](/wiki/Hellenic_National_Meteorological_Service "Hellenic National Meteorological Service"), South East Rhodes records the highest mean annual sunshine in Greece with over 3,100 hours.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

## Archaeology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rhodes&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Archaeology")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Kameiros_-_Fountain_square_03.jpg/250px-Kameiros_-_Fountain_square_03.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kameiros_-_Fountain_square_03.jpg)

Fountain square at the ancient site of [Kameiros](/wiki/Kameiros "Kameiros")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/KastroMonolithouMay112023_03.jpg/250px-KastroMonolithouMay112023_03.jpg)](/wiki/File:KastroMonolithouMay112023_03.jpg)

Medieval castle of [Monolithos](/wiki/Monolithos,_Greece "Monolithos, Greece")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/KastroKritiniasRhodesMay112023_12.jpg/250px-KastroKritiniasRhodesMay112023_12.jpg)](/wiki/File:KastroKritiniasRhodesMay112023_12.jpg)

Kastro Kritinias, [Kritinia](/wiki/Kritinia "Kritinia") Castle, The Kastellos

The [Colossus of Rhodes](/wiki/Colossus_of_Rhodes "Colossus of Rhodes") was considered to be one of the [Seven Wonders of the Ancient World](/wiki/Seven_Wonders_of_the_Ancient_World "Seven Wonders of the Ancient World"). This giant bronze statue was documented as once standing at the harbour. It was completed in 280 BC and destroyed in an [earthquake in 226 BC](/wiki/226_BC_Rhodes_earthquake "226 BC Rhodes earthquake"). No trace of the statue remains today.

Historical sites on the island of Rhodes include the [Acropolis of Lindos](/wiki/Lindos "Lindos"), the [Acropolis of Rhodes](/wiki/Acropolis_of_Rhodes "Acropolis of Rhodes") with the Temple of Pythian Apollo and an ancient theatre and stadium,[[72]](#cite_note-72) ancient [Ialysos](/wiki/Ialysos "Ialysos"), ancient [Kamiros](/wiki/Kamiros "Kamiros"), the [Governor's Palace](/w/index.php?title=Governor%27s_Palace_on_Rhodes&action=edit&redlink=1 "Governor's Palace on Rhodes (page does not exist)"), [Rhodes Old Town](/wiki/Fortifications_of_Rhodes "Fortifications of Rhodes") (walled medieval city), the [Palace of the Grand Masters](/wiki/Palace_of_the_Grand_Master_of_the_Knights_of_Rhodes "Palace of the Grand Master of the Knights of Rhodes"), [Kahal Shalom Synagogue](/wiki/Kahal_Shalom_Synagogue "Kahal Shalom Synagogue") in the [Jewish Quarter](/wiki/La_Juderia "La Juderia"), the [Archaeological Museum](/wiki/Archaeological_Museum_of_Rhodes "Archaeological Museum of Rhodes"), the ruins of the [castle of Monolithos](/wiki/Monolithos,_Greece "Monolithos, Greece"), the [castle of Kritinia](/wiki/Kritinia "Kritinia"), [St. Catherine Hospice](/w/index.php?title=St._Catherine_Hospice&action=edit&redlink=1 "St. Catherine Hospice (page does not exist)") and [Rhodes Footbridge](/wiki/Rhodes_Footbridge "Rhodes Footbridge").

## Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rhodes&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Religion")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Rhodes_Filerimos3_tango7174.jpg/250px-Rhodes_Filerimos3_tango7174.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rhodes_Filerimos3_tango7174.jpg)

Filerimos Monastery in [Ialysos](/wiki/Ialysos "Ialysos")

### Christianity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rhodes&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Christianity")]

The predominant religion of Rhodes is [Greek Orthodox](/wiki/Greek_Orthodox "Greek Orthodox"); the island is the seat of the [Metropolis of Rhodes](/wiki/Metropolis_of_Rhodes "Metropolis of Rhodes").

There is a [Latin Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Church "Roman Catholic Church")[[73]](#cite_note-73) minority on the island of 2,000, many of whom are descendants of Italians who remained after the end of the Italian occupation, pastorally served by the [Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Rhodes](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Rhodes "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Rhodes").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Islam

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rhodes&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Islam")]

Main article: [Turks of the Dodecanese](/wiki/Turks_of_the_Dodecanese "Turks of the Dodecanese")

Rhodes has a [Turkish](/wiki/Turkish_people "Turkish people") Muslim minority, which includes [Greek Muslims](/wiki/Greek_Muslims "Greek Muslims") whose ancestors from Crete and the Dodecanese converted to Islam in the Ottoman period[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]. Although a remnant from [Ottoman Turkish](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") times they were not required in the [population exchange](/wiki/Population_exchange_between_Greece_and_Turkey "Population exchange between Greece and Turkey") of 1923–24 to resettle in Turkey like the Turkish, Greek, and other Muslim communities living mainly in [Macedonia](/wiki/Macedonia_(Greece) "Macedonia (Greece)") and other parts of Northern Greece because unlike these areas the [Dodecanese Islands](/wiki/Dodecanese "Dodecanese") were under Italian administration at the time. They are organized around the Turkish Association of Rhodes ([Turkish](/wiki/Turkish_language "Turkish language"): *Rodos Türk Derneği*), which gives the figure 3,500 for the population they bring together and represent for the island.[[74]](#cite_note-74) The number of the Turks in Rhodes could be as many as 4,000.[[75]](#cite_note-75)[[76]](#cite_note-76)[[77]](#cite_note-77)

### Judaism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rhodes&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Judaism")]

Main article: [History of the Jews in Rhodes](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Rhodes "History of the Jews in Rhodes")

See also: [Selahattin Ülkümen](/wiki/Selahattin_%C3%9Clk%C3%BCmen "Selahattin Ülkümen")

The Jewish community of Rhodes[[78]](#cite_note-78) goes back 2,300 years.[[37]](#cite_note-auto-37) [Kahal Shalom Synagogue](/wiki/Kahal_Shalom_Synagogue "Kahal Shalom Synagogue"), established in 1557, during the [Ottoman](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") era, is the oldest synagogue in Greece and still stands in the Jewish quarter (*[La Juderia](/wiki/La_Juderia "La Juderia")*) of the old town of Rhodes. At its peak in the 1920s, the Jewish community was one-third of the town's total population.[[79]](#cite_note-79) In the 1940s, there were about 2000 Jews of various ethnic backgrounds. The Nazis deported and killed most of the community during the [Holocaust](/wiki/The_Holocaust "The Holocaust"). *Kahal Shalom* has been renovated with the help of foreign donors but few Jews live year-round in Rhodes today, so services are not held on a regular basis.[[80]](#cite_note-80)

The [Jewish Museum of Rhodes](/wiki/Jewish_Museum_of_Rhodes "Jewish Museum of Rhodes") was established in 1997 to preserve the Jewish history and culture of the Jews of Rhodes. It is adjacent to the Kahal Shalom Synagogue.

The Congolese businessman and politician, and former governor of Katanga, [Moïse Katumbi](/wiki/Mo%C3%AFse_Katumbi_Chapwe "Moïse Katumbi Chapwe")'s father, Nissim Soriano was a Greek [Sephardic Jew](/wiki/Sephardi_Jews "Sephardi Jews"), who fled Rhodes in 1938, who settled in [Katanga](/wiki/Katanga_Province "Katanga Province"), in the [Congo](/wiki/Belgian_Congo "Belgian Congo"), a Belgian colony at the time.[[81]](#cite_note-Versi13-81)

The present municipality Rhodes was formed at the 2011 local government reform by the merger of the following 10 former municipalities, that became municipal units (constituent communities in parentheses):[[1]](#cite_note-Kallikratis-1)

* [Afantou](/wiki/Afantou "Afantou") (Afantou, Archipoli)
* [Archangelos](/wiki/Archangelos,_Rhodes "Archangelos, Rhodes") (Archangelos, Malonas, Masari)
* [Attavyros](/wiki/Attavyros_(municipality) "Attavyros (municipality)") (Embonas, Kritinia, Monolithos, Siana, Agios Isidoros)
* [Ialysos](/wiki/Ialysos "Ialysos")
* [Kallithea](/wiki/Kallithea,_Rhodes "Kallithea, Rhodes") (Kalythies, Koskinou, Psinthos)
* [Kameiros](/wiki/Kameiros_(municipality) "Kameiros (municipality)") (Soroni, Apollona, Dimylia, [Kalavarda](/wiki/Kalavarda "Kalavarda"), Platania, Salakos, Fanes)
* [Lindos](/wiki/Lindos "Lindos") ([Lindos](/wiki/Lindos "Lindos"), Kalathos, Laerma, Lardos, Pylona)
* [Petaloudes](/wiki/Petaloudes "Petaloudes") (Kremasti, Pastida, Maritsa, Paradeisi, Theologos, Damatria)
* [Rhodes](/wiki/Rhodes_(city) "Rhodes (city)")
* [South Rhodes](/wiki/South_Rhodes "South Rhodes") (Gennadi, Apolakkia, Arnitha, Asklipieio, Vati, Istrios, Kattavia, Lachania,[[82]](#cite_note-82) [Mesanagros](/wiki/Mesanagros "Mesanagros"), Profilia)

The municipality has an area of 1400.681 km2.[[83]](#cite_note-stat01-83)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] It covers the island of Rhodes and a few uninhabited offshore islets. Rhodes city was the capital of the former [Dodecanese](/wiki/Dodecanese "Dodecanese") Prefecture. Rhodes is the most populated island of the [South Aegean](/wiki/South_Aegean "South Aegean") Region.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rhodes&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Economy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Hafenmarkt_Rhodos01.jpg/250px-Hafenmarkt_Rhodos01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hafenmarkt_Rhodos01.jpg)

View of the market (Nea Agora) of Mandraki (Rhodes city), built during the Italian period

The economy is tourist-oriented, and the most developed sector is service. Tourism has elevated Rhodes economically, compared to the rest of Greece.[[84]](#cite_note-84)

Small industries process imported raw materials for local retail, though other industry includes agricultural goods production, stockbreeding, fishery and winery.

## Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rhodes&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Transportation")]

### Air

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rhodes&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Air")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Airpoort_rhodes_02.JPG/250px-Airpoort_rhodes_02.JPG)](/wiki/File:Airpoort_rhodes_02.JPG)

Diagoras Airport, arrivals terminal

Rhodes has two airports, but only one is public. [Diagoras Airport](/wiki/Diagoras_Airport "Diagoras Airport"), southwest of [Rhodes City](/wiki/Rhodes_City "Rhodes City"), is the fourth biggest by passenger volume in Greece, and the main entrance/exit point to the island for both locals and tourists. The island is well connected with other major Greek cities and islands as well as with major European capitals and cities via charter flights. Until 1977, [Rhodes Maritsa Airport](/wiki/Rhodes_Maritsa_Airport "Rhodes Maritsa Airport"), built in 1938, was a public airport; it is now used by the [Hellenic Air Force](/wiki/Hellenic_Air_Force "Hellenic Air Force") and occasionally for car races.

There are also two inoperative airfields. Kalathos Airfield, north of [Lindos](/wiki/Lindos "Lindos"), and Kattavia Airstrip, to the south of the island, were built by the Italians during the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"). Neither remains operational.

Two pilot schools offer aviation services (small plane rental and island hopping).

### Sea

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rhodes&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Sea")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/Louis_Majesty_Rhodes_2.jpg/250px-Louis_Majesty_Rhodes_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Louis_Majesty_Rhodes_2.jpg)

[*Louis Majesty*](/wiki/MS_Crown_Iris "MS Crown Iris") at the harbour of Rhodes

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/Rhodes_SkalaKamirou_tango7174.jpg/250px-Rhodes_SkalaKamirou_tango7174.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rhodes_SkalaKamirou_tango7174.jpg)

The Kameiros Skala Dock

Rhodes has five ports, three of them in [Rhodes City](/wiki/Rhodes_City "Rhodes City"), one in the west coast near [Kamiros](/wiki/Kamiros "Kamiros") and one in east coast near [Lardos](/wiki/Lardos "Lardos").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

* Central Port: located in the city of Rhodes serves exclusively international traffic consisting of scheduled services to/from Turkey, cruise ships and yachts. Since Summer 2012, the port is also a homeport for Costa Cruises during the summer period.
* Kolona Port: opposite and north of the central port, serves intra-Dodecanese traffic and all sizes yachts.
* Akandia Port: the new port of the island, south and next to the central port, being built since the 1960s, for domestic, cargo and general purpose traffic. Since 2017 summer a passenger terminal is finally in use hosting a cafe and waiting lounges.[[85]](#cite_note-85)
* Mandraki Port: the oldest port of the island, in the center of Rhodes city. Many cruise boats begin their daily trips to Symi island or to the southern east coast until Lindos.[[86]](#cite_note-86)
* Kamiros Skala Dock: 30 km (19 mi) south west of the city near [Ancient Kamiros](/wiki/Ancient_Kamiros "Ancient Kamiros") ruins serves mainly the island of [Halki](/wiki/Halki_(Greece) "Halki (Greece)").
* Lardos Dock: formerly servicing local industries, now under development as an alternative port for times when the central port is inaccessible due to weather conditions. It is situated in a rocky shore near the village of Lardos in south east Rhodes.

### Road network

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rhodes&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Road network")]

The road network of the island is mostly paved and consists of 3 national roads plus one planned, 40 provincial and numerous local. These are the four major island arteries:

* [Rhodes-Kamiros Province Avenue](/w/index.php?title=Rhodes-Kamiros_Province_Avenue&action=edit&redlink=1 "Rhodes-Kamiros Province Avenue (page does not exist)"): Province road 2 till [Kalavarda](/wiki/Kalavarda "Kalavarda") village and 21 from there till [Kamiros](/wiki/Kamiros "Kamiros") with two lane that runs through the west coast north to south and connects Rhodes City with [Diagoras Airport](/wiki/Diagoras_Airport "Diagoras Airport") and [Kamiros](/wiki/Kamiros "Kamiros").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* Rhodes-Lindos National Avenue ([Greek National Road 95](/wiki/Greek_National_Road_95 "Greek National Road 95")): Four and two lane, runs mainly inland north to south and connects Rhodes City with Lindos.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Part from Rhodes Town until Kolympia is now 4 lanes, the rest until Lindos is 2 lanes.
* Rhodes-Kallithea-Faliraki Province Avenue 4: Two lanes, runs through the east coast north to south and connects Rhodes City with Kallithea monument and Faliraki Resort.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* Tsairi-Airport National Avenue ([Greek National Road 100](/w/index.php?title=Greek_National_Road_100&action=edit&redlink=1 "Greek National Road 100 (page does not exist)")): Four and two lane, runs inland east to west and connects the east coast with the west and the airport.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* Lindos-Katavia Province Road 1: Two lane, begins just before Lindos and though villages and resorts leads to Katavia village, the southernmost of the island, from where a further deviation leads to Prasonissi.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* Rhodes Town [Ring Road](/wiki/Ring_Road "Ring Road") (Phase 1): Beginning from the new marina and ending to Rhodes-Kallithea province avenue is a four lane expressway.

Future roads:[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

* Further widening of Rhodes-Lindos National Avenue ([Greek National Road 95](/wiki/Greek_National_Road_95 "Greek National Road 95")) from Kolympia to Lindos. This is to be four lane with a [jersey barrier](/wiki/Jersey_barrier "Jersey barrier") in the middle. A tender is expected to take place by end of 2019 so as constructions can begin.
* Ring Road phases 2, 3, and 4 pending; phase 2 will extend the expressway to [Greek National Road 95](/wiki/Greek_National_Road_95 "Greek National Road 95") and then to Rhodes General Hospital where it supposedly will connect to also planned new Rhodes City-Airport expressway. In June 2018 Rhodes municipality stated that plans for the final 700 meters of the ring road leading to Akandia Port are pending approval.[[87]](#cite_note-87) Phases 3 and 4 which plan to run the ring road from hospital hill down to Ixia and then through Kritika back to the town will most probably never occur.
* Plans also exist for a new four lane express road connecting Rhodes Town with [Diagoras Airport](/wiki/Diagoras_Airport "Diagoras Airport"). The road, recognised as National back in 2014,[[88]](#cite_note-88) will follow existing Provincial Road 3 routing with a total length of 8.6 km and including 3 main junctions and is intended to relieve congestion on the coastal west avenue. The so-called Leoforos Mesogeion is vastly anticipated and is a top priority for local authorities.

### Bus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rhodes&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Bus")]

Bus services are handled by two operators:[[89]](#cite_note-89)

* RODA: Municipal bus company that serves Rhodes city as well suburban areas (Koskinou, Faliraki, Ialysos, Kremasti, Airport, Pastida, Maritsa, Paradeisi) and the west coast of the island
* KTEL: Limited liability private transport company that serve villages and resorts in the east coast of the island

### Cars and motorbikes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rhodes&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Cars and motorbikes")]

Families in Rhodes often own more than one car, along with a motorbike. Traffic jams are common particularly in the summer months as vehicles more than double while parking spots downtown and around the old town are limited and can't cope with demand. Moreover, the island is served by 450 taxis and some 200 public and private buses adding to the traffic burden.

## Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rhodes&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Sports")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/Rhodos003.JPG/250px-Rhodos003.JPG)](/wiki/File:Rhodos003.JPG)

[Diagoras Stadium](/wiki/Diagoras_Stadium "Diagoras Stadium") in the city of Rhodes

* Football: [AS Rodos](/wiki/AS_Rodos "AS Rodos") and [Diagoras F.C.](/wiki/Diagoras_F.C. "Diagoras F.C.") are the island's biggest teams and rivals. The latter competed in the 2018–19 season at the national level third tier ([Gamma Ethniki](/wiki/Gamma_Ethniki "Gamma Ethniki")) along with [GAS Ialysos](/wiki/GAS_Ialysos_1948_F.C. "GAS Ialysos 1948 F.C.") and both achieved promotion to ([Greek Football League](/wiki/Greek_Football_League "Greek Football League")). [AS Rodos](/wiki/AS_Rodos "AS Rodos") competed in 1st tier of the local league and ranking 1st achieved promotion and returned after one year to ([Gamma Ethniki](/wiki/Gamma_Ethniki "Gamma Ethniki")) which in the 2019–20 season became tier 4. Local football leagues (organized at the prefecture-level) contain three divisions with more than 50 teams.[[90]](#cite_note-90) Many stadiums are grass-covered.[[91]](#cite_note-91)
* Basketball: [Colossus BC](/wiki/Kolossos_Rodou_BC "Kolossos Rodou BC") sponsors professional basketball and after more than a decade of presence in the top-level [Greek Basket League](/wiki/Greek_Basket_League "Greek Basket League") was relegated to [Greek A2 Basket League](/wiki/Greek_A2_Basket_League "Greek A2 Basket League"). The local league includes a single division with two groups, one for Rhodes and the other for the other islands, with 7 and 5 teams respectively.[[92]](#cite_note-92) Three indoor courts exist in Rhodes City, and one each in Ialysos, Kremasti, and Faliraki. [Archangelos](/wiki/Archangelos,_Rhodes "Archangelos, Rhodes") town will also get an indoor court according to Rhodes municipality planned works and regional government's approved funds.[[93]](#cite_note-93)
* Volleyball: Rodion Athlisis managed to escape local obscurity and until 2018–19 season competed at the national level second-tier failing to achieve promotion to the first level in playoffs for three consecutive seasons.[[94]](#cite_note-94) This unlucky streak caused team sponsors to withdraw from the men's team and focus solely on developing youth academies.[[95]](#cite_note-95)
* Water polo: mostly amateur-based. There is not any single public indoor pool on the island.
* Rugby: introduced in 2007. Teams compete at the national level.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* Tennis: Rhodes Tennis Club (Ροδιακός Όμιλος Αντισφαίρισης) promotes officially tennis since 1949. Club operates on two separate locations, one downtown next to the casino and one next to [Kallipateira National Athletic Centre](/w/index.php?title=Kallipateira_National_Athletic_Centre&action=edit&redlink=1 "Kallipateira National Athletic Centre (page does not exist)").[[96]](#cite_note-96)
* Sailing: Island has competed at the international level[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* Cycling: For a long period of time Rhodes had the only velodrome in Greece. For the moment, the island is the seat of the Dodecanissos Local Cycling Committee. Most notable cycling clubs are [Rodilios CC](/w/index.php?title=Rodilios_CC&action=edit&redlink=1 "Rodilios CC (page does not exist)"), [Diagoras GC](/w/index.php?title=Diagoras_GC&action=edit&redlink=1 "Diagoras GC (page does not exist)"), [Elafos CC](/w/index.php?title=Elafos_CC&action=edit&redlink=1 "Elafos CC (page does not exist)"), [Iviskos CC](/w/index.php?title=Iviskos_CC&action=edit&redlink=1 "Iviskos CC (page does not exist)"), all based in the city of Rhodes, plus [Antaios SC](/w/index.php?title=Antaios_SC&action=edit&redlink=1 "Antaios SC (page does not exist)") of [Kremasti](/wiki/Kremasti "Kremasti") and [Athlos SC](/w/index.php?title=Athlos_SC&action=edit&redlink=1 "Athlos SC (page does not exist)") of [Paradeisi](/wiki/Paradeisi "Paradeisi"). In Rhodes, the [International Tour of Rhodes](/wiki/International_Tour_of_Rhodes "International Tour of Rhodes"), part of [UCI Europe Tour](/wiki/UCI_Europe_Tour "UCI Europe Tour") Cycling Calendar, is annually organized.
* Rhodes competed in the bi-annual [Island Games](/wiki/International_Island_Games_Association "International Island Games Association"), which it hosted in 2007.[[97]](#cite_note-97) Since 2019 is suspended from competition.

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rhodes&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Cuisine")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/Pitaroudia.jpg/250px-Pitaroudia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pitaroudia.jpg)

*Pitaroudia*, a traditional chickpea dumpling from Rhodes and Dodecanese

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/Fanouropita.jpg/250px-Fanouropita.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fanouropita.jpg)

[Fanouropita](/wiki/Fanouropita "Fanouropita")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/%CE%9C%CE%B5%CE%BB%CE%B5%CE%BA%CE%BF%CF%8D%CE%BD%CE%B9.jpg/250px-%CE%9C%CE%B5%CE%BB%CE%B5%CE%BA%CE%BF%CF%8D%CE%BD%CE%B9.jpg)](/wiki/File:%CE%9C%CE%B5%CE%BB%CE%B5%CE%BA%CE%BF%CF%8D%CE%BD%CE%B9.jpg)

Melekouni

Rhodian tradition in cuisine is rich. [Koriantolino](/w/index.php?title=Koriantolino&action=edit&redlink=1 "Koriantolino (page does not exist)") and [Souma](/w/index.php?title=Souma_(Greek_cuisine)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Souma (Greek cuisine) (page does not exist)") (colorless alcoholic beverage produced from grape distillation) are the main alcoholic drinks of Rhodes. Local foods include:

* Escharitis, type of bread
* Pitaroudia
* Milla and Tsiriggia, meat fat
* [Pougia](/w/index.php?title=Pougia&action=edit&redlink=1 "Pougia (page does not exist)") pie
* Lakani, goat meat with chickpeas
* Lópia (beans) with goat
* Matsi, hand made pasta used to make [Koulouría](/wiki/Koulourakia "Koulourakia"), a traditional recipe
* Synoro, traditional cheese
* [Tahinopita](/wiki/Tahinopita "Tahinopita")
* Zvigoi, type of [loukoumades](/wiki/Loukoumades "Loukoumades")
* [Melekouni](/w/index.php?title=Melekouni&action=edit&redlink=1 "Melekouni (page does not exist)")
* [Fanouropita](/wiki/Fanouropita "Fanouropita")
* Takakia (Mantinades)
* Katimeria (tiganites, pancakes)
* [Amygdalota](/wiki/Amygdalota "Amygdalota"), white almond cookies
* Moschopougia

* [Braith Anasta](/wiki/Braith_Anasta "Braith Anasta") (born 1982), rugby league player and [NRL](/wiki/NRL "NRL") premiership winner, ancestral ties to the island through his father
* [Stergos Felegakis](/wiki/Stergos_Felegakis "Stergos Felegakis") (born 1986), professional football player
* [Nick Galis](/wiki/Nick_Galis "Nick Galis") (born 1957), basketball player, [FIBA Hall of Fame](/wiki/FIBA_Hall_of_Fame "FIBA Hall of Fame") and [Naismith Memorial Basketball Hall of Fame](/wiki/Naismith_Memorial_Basketball_Hall_of_Fame "Naismith Memorial Basketball Hall of Fame") member (his father was born in a small village called [Agios Isidoros](/wiki/Agios_Isidoros,_Rhodes "Agios Isidoros, Rhodes"))
* [Niki Xanthou](/wiki/Niki_Xanthou "Niki Xanthou") (born 1973), long jumper

## Tourism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rhodes&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Tourism")]

Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(December 2022)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

Rhodes is one of the most attractive tourist destinations in Greece. After [Crete](/wiki/Crete "Crete"), the island is the most visited destination in Greece, with arrivals standing at 1,785,305 in 2013. In 2014 they stood at 1,931,005, while in 2015 the arrival number reduced slightly and stood at 1,901,000.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The average length of stay is estimated at 8 days. Guests from Great Britain, Israel, France, Italy, Sweden and Norway constitute the biggest portion in terms of the arrivals by country. In Rhodes, the supply of available rooms is high, since more than 550 hotels are operating in the island, the majority of which are two star hotels.

## In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rhodes&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

* "[Hic Rhodus, hic salta!](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/hic_Rhodus,_hic_salta "wikt:hic Rhodus, hic salta")", a phrase from antiquity
* The *[Hellenic Traders](/wiki/Hellenic_Traders "Hellenic Traders")* series of historical novels by Harry Turtledove centers around the adventures of a trading galley based in Rhodes during the 4th century BCE.
* Movies shot on the island include *[The Guns of Navarone](/wiki/The_Guns_of_Navarone_(film) "The Guns of Navarone (film)")* (1961), *[Kiss the Girls](/wiki/Kiss_the_Girls_(1965_film) "Kiss the Girls (1965 film)")* by [Giannis Dalianidis](/wiki/Giannis_Dalianidis "Giannis Dalianidis") (1965), and *[Escape to Athena](/wiki/Escape_to_Athena "Escape to Athena")* (1979). Rhodes was also the setting of Agatha Christie's "Triangle at Rhodes".
